title: What is a variable
instruction: A variable is a labeled slot that holds a value — like a shelf slot labeled with a book title. Run this to see one in action.
successMessage: You stored a book title and retrieved it. That's what every variable in every library system does.

title: Create your own with :=
instruction: `:=` creates a variable and gives it a value in one shot. Change the title to your favorite book and run it.
hint: Replace "The Hobbit" with your favorite book title. Keep the quotes.

codeChecks.message: Change the value from "The Hobbit" to your own favorite book title.

successMessage: Your first original variable. := creates it and fills it in one shot.

title: Declare first, assign later
instruction: `var title string` creates an empty slot. `title = "The Hobbit"` puts a book in it. Note: filling later uses `=`, not `:=`. Run this.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var title string
	title = "The Hobbit"
	fmt.Println(title)
}
successMessage: Two separate steps: create the shelf slot, then put the book on it.

title: Update a variable — the aha 🤯
instruction: Variables can change. `:=` creates, `=` updates. The count starts at 60. A shipment arrives — update it to 95 and print both values.
hint: After the first Println, write: copies = 95 then fmt.Println(copies) again.

codeChecks.message: Update copies using a single = : copies = 95
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Same shelf slot, new count. = refills. := creates. Never mix them up.

title: Two variables in one line
instruction: Go lets you declare multiple variables at once: `title, author := "The Hobbit", "Tolkien"`. Change the book and author to your own.
hint: Replace the values with your own book title and author.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title, author := "The Hobbit", "Tolkien"
	fmt.Println(title)
	fmt.Println(author)
}

codeChecks.message: Keep the multi-assignment format: title, author := ...
Change the book title and author to your own.

successMessage: One line, two variables. Left to right: title gets first, author gets second.

title: Zero values
instruction: What happens if you create a shelf slot but never put a book in? Go gives it a default called a zero value. Run this to see them.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var title string
	var copies int
	var available bool
	fmt.Println(title)
	fmt.Println(copies)
	fmt.Println(available)
}
successMessage: No surprises in Go. Every variable always has a value, even before you catalog a single book.

title: Build a library profile
instruction: Use everything you learned: create `title` and `copies` with `:=`, declare `section` with `var` and assign it separately, then print all three.
hint: title := "...", copies := 10, var section string then section = "...". Then print them.

codeChecks.message: Declare section using the var keyword

successMessage: You used every form of variable declaration in Go. Next up: the fmt package.
